solution: Numerator and denominator have equal degree (2), so the end behavior is the ratio of the leading coefficients: \( \lim_{x \to \infty} = \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This is the value when the numerator's degree is smaller, which is not the case here.
This assumes the ratio approaches 1.
This multiplies the leading coefficients instead of dividing them.
This adds the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: The denominator's degree (\( [[n]] \)) exceeds the numerator's degree (1), so the denominator grows faster and the ratio is driven to \( 0 \): \( \lim_{x \to \infty} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the degrees are equal and takes the ratio of leading coefficients.
This assumes the ratio approaches 1.
This reports only the numerator's leading coefficient.
This uses the ratio of the constant terms instead of recognizing the limit is 0.
This multiplies the leading coefficients instead of recognizing the limit is 0.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Each factored binomial contributes one degree, so both the numerator and denominator are degree \( 2 \); expanding, the leading term of the numerator is \( [[a]]x^{2} \) and of the denominator is \( [[b]]x^{2} \). The specific roots \( r_1,\dots,r_4 \) don't affect the end behavior: \( \lim_{x\to\infty} = \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the leading coefficients.
This mistakenly assumes the denominator has higher degree.
This assumes the ratio approaches 1.
This multiplies the leading coefficients instead of dividing them.
This uses the ratio of the roots' products instead of the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]x^{3} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]])}{[[b]]x^{2} + ([[e]])x - 2} \). Since the numerator's degree exceeds the denominator's by exactly \( 1 \), \( f \) has no horizontal asymptote, but \( \dfrac{f(x)}{x} \) does approach a finite limit (the slope of \( f \)'s slant asymptote). Evaluate \( \lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{f(x)}{x} \).
solution: Dividing out the extra degree, \( \dfrac{f(x)}{x} = \dfrac{[[a]]x^{3}+\cdots}{[[b]]x^{3}+\cdots} \) is now a ratio of equal-degree (3) polynomials, so it tends to the ratio of leading coefficients: \( \lim_{x\to\infty}\dfrac{f(x)}{x} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This forgets to divide by the extra factor of \( x \), giving the (nonexistent) limit of \( f(x) \) itself as if it converged to 0.
This reports only the numerator's leading coefficient.
This multiplies the leading coefficients instead of dividing them.
This adds the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
